summary: The test does exercise real `runAutomation`, `scopedSecrets`, and Google `runAction`, and its self-heal path does require the fixer loop. But its strongest claims are still false-green on redaction/leak-proofing, and it bypasses the production vault->connector integration path that the prose says is proven end-to-end.

findings.title: The 'token never leaks' check never forces redaction to do any work, so it can pass even if scrubbing is broken
findings.recommendation: Make at least one persisted/emitted/logged payload actually contain the secret before redaction, then assert the persisted/emitted form is redacted. As written, `emit` only sees engine events, which never include `authEnv`, and `logSink` only appends redacted events plus the Gmail API result, which also does not contain the token. This means the assertions can pass simply because the token was never serialized into those structures in the first place.

findings.title: The test bypasses the production connector-auth and connector-executor integration path, so it does not prove the claimed vault->scoped connector delivery end-to-end
findings.recommendation: If the claim is specifically that Garrison's real connector delivery path is proven, avoid overriding `connectorAuthEnv` and `runConnector`, or add a narrower claim. The current test directly calls `scopedSecrets(["GOOGLE_ACCESS_TOKEN"])` and directly invokes `googleRunAction(...)`, which does prove `vault -> scopedSecrets -> google runAction -> Authorization header`, but it does not exercise the engine's default `/api/connectors/:id/auth-env` path or spawned connector process contract.
